retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues
retrieved_chunks.content:
In addition, the RPOs are responsible for processing Chapter 32 refunds for Veterans within its jurisdiction. This includes death refund development and payments.

(1) Handling requests for general information. (ROs should send inquiries regarding specific claims and related information to RPOs.)

(2) Provide character of service determinations for Veterans with service not characterized as Honorable or Under Honorable Conditions

NOTE: Veteran Claims Examiners (VCEs) should follow local RPO procedures regarding how to send a request to a RO.

retrieved_chunks.title: PART 01, GENERAL. CHAPTER 04. PROCEDURES FOR REGIONALIZED PROCESSING
heading_path: PART 01, GENERAL. CHAPTER 04. PROCEDURES FOR REGIONALIZED PROCESSING
retrieved_chunks.content:
1. Original Claims. If a local RO receives an original claim on VA Form 22-1990, send the document to the appropriate RPO.

2. Requests for Transfer-Out of Education or Chapter 30 Folders. Only the following types of stations should ever request a transfer of an education or a chapter 30 folder from the RPO: other RPOs, VA Central Office, the local RO requiring review of an education appellate issue by the claimant's designated representative before certification, the BVA (Board of Veterans Appeals), or the COVA (Court of Veterans Appeals). When the RPO receives a request for transfer-out of an education folder from a local RO, the RPO must check by telephone (or electronic mail) with the requesting station to see what type of document the requesting station has received.

i. If the requesting station has an education document, the RPO will advise the station to refer that document to the RPO

ii. If the requesting station has a document relating to a different benefit, i.e., C&P, REPS, or chapter 31, the RPO should advise the requesting station that the RPO does not have jurisdiction over that benefit (c) If the requesting station has a valid reason for requesting the education or the chapter 30 folder, the RPO will make a temporary transfer.

3. Changes of Address. All ROs will process changes of address (including establishing and changing direct deposits for education claimants. (See PART 01, GENERAL. CHAPTER 10, DIRECT DEPOSIT, for direct deposit information.)

retrieved_chunks.title: M22-4 Addendum | Glossary - Appendix B: Common Terminology
heading_path: M22-4 Addendum | Glossary - Appendix B: Common Terminology
retrieved_chunks.content:
Appendix B.  Common Terminology

REGIONAL OFFICE (RO)

Relatively large VA field office that generally includes staff from all of the VBA elements - Director's Office, Customer Service Center (Adjudication and Veterans Services), Loan Guaranty, Vocational Rehabilitation and Counseling, and Support Services.

REGIONAL PROCESSING OFFICE (RPO)

VA offices that process Education claims. Currently, there are two RPOs – Buffalo, NY (RO 307) and Muskogee, OK (RO 351).

RELEASED FROM ACTIVE DUTY (RAD)

Date a Veteran is released from active duty.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 6: Chapter 4 - Award Processing and Other Authorization Issues
heading_path: Part 6: Chapter 4 - Award Processing and Other Authorization Issues
SUBCHAPTER IV.  OTHER AWARD PROCESSING ISSUES
retrieved_chunks.content:
Go to Top

(1)  If a RO receives a VA Form 4-5281 from a claimant with no chapter 32 master record, take the following actions: 

(i)  Review for Chapter 30, Category III.  If BDN shows that the veteran is on active duty or discharged after February 3, 1991, take the following actions:

A.  Check BDN to determine if there is a chapter 30 master record or a pending claim for chapter 30.  If so, send the refund request to the RPO. 

B.  If the veteran does not have a chapter 30 master record or a pending claim, have the RO Finance activity process the refund request. 

(ii)  Review for Chapter 30, Category IVA.  If BDN shows that the veteran is on active duty or discharged after October 9, 1996, send the refund request to the RPO. 

NOTE 1:  The refund request may specifically mention "chapter 30 election," Public Law 104-275 or something similar.  RO personnel should also be familiar with circular 22-97-1 and appendix A to that circular.  These procedures pertain to chapter 32 participants still on active duty as of October 9, 1996 who qualify for an election to chapter 30.  Send these refund requests to the RPO.

NOTE 2:  In certain instances, PPU has paid chapter 32 benefits.  In these instances, PPU will set a BDN file pull indicator that will prevent ROs from processing refund requests.  If the BDN master record show either the message "CH32 BENEFITS HAVE BEEN PAID" or "VETERAN HAS REC'D INTERIM SYSTEM BENEFITS" at the bottom of the M26-M29 screens, contact PPU before taking any further action on the refund request.  (See par. 4.06e.)  Ensure that PPU has completed their processing BEFORE refunding any contributions. 

(2)  If a regional office receives a VA Form 4-5281 from a claimant with a running or terminated chapter 32 master record, send the refund request to the RPO.  

NOTE:  ROs should continue to process refunds shown on the COIN EDU 296 CHAPTER 32 TRANSFER UNCLAIMED FUNDS TO TREASURY list.

results.query: What dollar threshold triggers four-signature review for a Chapter 33 school payment?

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER IV.  DELEGATION OF AUTHORITY FOR EDUCATION CLAIMS
retrieved_chunks.content:
NOTE: When the BDN payment screen exceeds award line limitation to deliver payments for an entire non-chapter 33 award, the RPO may use multiple end products, but only one original or one supplemental EP to authorize big payment awards. Subsequent end products must be a 930 EP.

NOTE: Under Chapter 33, a VCE may process a single payment to a school if it is less than $45,000 and may process a single payment to an individual if it is less than $25,000, without additional signatures.

d. LTS and BDN system limitations.  Due to multiple system limitations in both the Long Term Solution (LTS) and BDN, VCEs may find it necessary to split the award lines into multiple work products (WPs) to allow the proper payment(s) to release. VCEs should never use the practice of breaking up big payment awards on multiple work products to justify NOT requiring proper signature authorization

A big or large retroactive payment is an award where the cumulative payment reaches the threshold amounts.  As an example, if several single payments appear on the same award, the cumulative amount would, therefore, qualify as a big pay and must adhere to proper authorization procedures.  

LTS payments currently will fail if a payment over $99,999.99 is attempted. Due to this system limitation, it is acceptable to break the payments into multiple work products to ensure the payments are authorized. One of the payments must be over the threshold but under $99,999.99. The remaining payments may be under the threshold. However, the requirement for signature authorization remains for the entire cumulative total of payment due to the student or school.

EXAMPLE:   Chapter 33 Tuition and Fees (T&F) amount totals $120,000
                              First WP is authorized with T&F input as $95,000
                              Second WP is authorized with T&F corrected to $120,000 (releasing the additional $25,000)
                              Total T&F paid to school is $120,000

When processing chapter 33 claims with future maturation dates, and the award exceeds the threshold, process the claim following all big pay procedures.  After the award is authorized in LTS, suspend the claim in The Image Management System (TIMS) until the maturation date. (There is no need to establish a diary/future end product in BDN for additional control).  At this time, appropriate station and individual end product credit are authorized.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 1 - Administrative Issues
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER IV.  DELEGATION OF AUTHORITY FOR EDUCATION CLAIMS
retrieved_chunks.content:
Benefit(s)

Three Signatures Required

Four Signatures Required

Chapter 33

Not Applicable
  • Single payment to a school of $45,000 or more,
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $25,000 or more, OR
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $25,000 or more AND a portion will be offset by a debt.

Chapters 30 and 35
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $20,000 to $24,999.99.
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $25,000 or greater.

Chapters 1606 and 32
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $8,000 to $11,999.99.
  • Single payment to a beneficiary of $12,000 or greater.

retrieved_chunks.title: M22-4, Part II, Chapter 2, Section E - Reviewing Chapter 33 Payment History
heading_path: M22-4, Part II, Chapter 2, Section E - Reviewing Chapter 33 Payment History
retrieved_chunks.content:
Overview

This section discusses how to review the payment history of chapter 33 claims.

In This Section

This section contains the following topics:

Topic

Topic Name

1      

Topic 1: Reviewing BTs Issued on or after July 8, 2024

2      

Topic 2: Reviewing BTs Issued before July 8, 2024

Topic 1: Reviewing BTs Issued on or after July 8, 2024

Introduction

This section covers viewing payment records in the Enterprise Management of Payment Workload and Reporting (eMPWR) after the transition from BDN for Chapter 33 processing.

Change Date

June 24, 2025

II.2.E.1.a

General

Users who review Education Benefit payments under the Chapter 33 for payments issued on or after July 8, 2024, will do so using the eMPWR user interface in SalesForce.  For details on logging on and accessing eMPWR in SalesForce, please review the Logging into the Application section of the eMPWR User Guide.

Unless additional access is specifically needed for their position or role, individuals will be given access to the eMPWR system with an inquiry (read-only) role. Any fiscal transactions needed for the correction or completion of a Chapter 33 record must be routed for review and processing by the Regional Processing Office’s (RPO) Support Services Division (SSD).

Users can search for a participant (beneficiary) by SSN or file number.

After a successful participant search is conducted from the home screen, the participant record is displayed via Participant Record screen with tabs including Transaction History, Participant Details, Notes, and eFolder.
   

Figure: eMPWR Participant Search
 

Figure: eMPWR Participant Record screen

Note: Due to changes in how relationships are established for TOE beneficiaries, when payments need to be reviewed for a TOE beneficiary who was paid via both BDN and eMPWR, multiple File Number searches will be needed – one using the transferee’s file number (for payments made via BDN) and one using the transferor’s file number (for payments made via eMPWR).

II.2.E.1.b

Transaction History Tab

When processing a claim, responding to questions received via the telephone or written correspondence, Education employees require the ability to search the system to retrieve all transactions associated with the searched participant. The Transaction History tab contains the following information.

1.    All Transactions – displays all transaction history records, not just those transactions sent from Education service.

2.    Deductions – displays active Benefit Repayment and Offsets deduction records. The processor can change the default filter to display inactive deductions.

3.    Payments – displays all payments scheduled for release by treasury and the details of those payment records.

4.    Receivables – displays all active receivable records with a balance greater than zero. The processor can change the default filter to display inactive receivables.

After a successful participant search, the system redirects to the Transaction History tab on the Participant Record page, displaying the All Transactions default grid for Accountable Balances.

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – All Transactions example

 

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Payments example

Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Receivables example

From this view, the processor also can view the details of a transaction by clicking on the Transaction Id.  Details vary depending upon the transaction but can include the following.

1.    Account Details/Balance Summary (Receivables, Deductions, Accountable Balances)

a.    Status of the Transaction, including outstanding balances (if applicable)

2.    Payment Details (Payment transactions only)

3.    Notes (All transaction types)

a.      Any notes entered during transaction processing.

4.    Historical Transactions against the account (Receivables, Deductions, Accountable Balances)

a.      Any transactions made against the account.

5.    Associated Documents (Accountable Balances)

a.      If any documents were uploaded as part of establishing the account/transaction

6.    Payment Address (Payment transactions only)

7.    Details about payment address or EFT account.

The processor can select the gear icon to export the transaction details to Microsoft Excel CSV format.

  Figure: eMPWR Transaction History – Export

Select the Close button to exit the window and return to the Transaction History grid.

Go to Top

Topic 2: Reviewing BTs Issued before July 8, 2024

Introduction

This section covers the process to reviewing transactions released before the transition from BDN to eMPWR.

Change Date

June 24, 2025

II.2.E.2.a

General

While Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) can no longer establish (CEST) or adjust (CADJ) Chapter 33 Master Records in BDN upon the transition to eMPWR, VCEs still can view the master record and transaction history from before the conversion in BDN.  To do so, users will follow the processes found in M22-4, Part XII, 9.02 - 9.06. 

Note: All active account receivables have been migrated to eMPWR as part of the conversion.  As such, no information will display on the M01 Accounts Receivable screen.

Note: No transactions submitted on or after July 8, 2024, will display in BDN.  VCEs must review eMPWR to view these transactions. This means that both eMPWR and BDN will need to be reviewed if a beneficiary has activity both before and after July 8, 2024.

Go to Top

Next Section

retrieved_chunks.title: PART 1, GENERAL, CHAPTER 11, COMMITTEE ON WAIVERS AND COMPROMISES
heading_path: PART 1, GENERAL, CHAPTER 11, COMMITTEE ON WAIVERS AND COMPROMISES
retrieved_chunks.content:
1. Chapter 33 debts
  • 71B debts associated with books/supplies payments or other lump-sum payments to the beneficiary other than chapter 30 or chapter 1606 kickers. Supporting document codes include 11 through 15, 27, and 28.
  • 72B debts associated with housing payments which may include chapter 33 kickers and/or chapter 33 supplemental assistance. Supporting document codes include 16 through 20 and 48 through 51.
  • 73B debts associated with chapter 30 and/or chapter 1606 kickers paid under chapter 33. Supporting document codes include 76 through 79.
  • 74B debts associated with payments made to the school (including Yellow Ribbon) but are charged to the student when the student attended any portion of the enrollment. Supporting document codes include 52, 62, 80, and 86 through 90.
  • 75B debts associated with payments made to the school (including Yellow Ribbon) and charged to the school.  Supporting document codes include 63 and 91 through 98.

retrieved_chunks.title: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.6 - Reimbursements, Fees, and Overpayments
heading_path: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.6 - Reimbursements, Fees, and Overpayments
retrieved_chunks.content: • For an individual who does not complete one or more courses in the certified period of enrollment, and who has not established the existence of acceptable mitigating circumstances, the overpayment will be equal the total amount of educational assistance paid for the course or courses not completed during that certified period of enrollment.

  • 74B debts associated with payments made to the school (including Yellow Ribbon) but are charged to the student when the student attended any portion of the enrollment. Supporting document codes include 52, 62, 80, and 86 through 90.
  • 75B debts associated with payments made to the school (including Yellow Ribbon) and charged to the school.  Supporting document codes include 63 and 91 through 98.

  • For an individual who does not complete one or more courses in the certified period of enrollment, and who has not established the existence of acceptable mitigating circumstances, the overpayment will be equal the total amount of educational assistance paid for the course or courses not completed during that certified period of enrollment.

retrieved_chunks.title: VADIR Chapter 33 Long Term Solution Web Service Interface Control Document v2.1.1
retrieved_chunks.content:
Chapter 33 of the Montgomery GI Bill (MGIB) is an education benefits program created by Congress in July 2008. Formally titled the Post/911 Montgomery GI Bill, Chapter 33 provides education benefits for service members and Veterans who have served on Active Duty for 90 or more days since September 10, 2001. Veterans who qualify for Chapter 33 benefits are eligible to receive up to 100% payment of tuition and fees for education and training programs taken through accredited colleges or universities; a monthly housing stipend; up to $1,000 a year for books and supplies; a relocation allowance; and reimbursement for certification, licensing, work-study programs, or tutorial assistance. After meeting specific eligibility requirements, Veterans can also transfer their unused benefits to their spouses and dependents.  
The Chapter 33 Long Term Solution (LTS) is a web service that automates the processing of the complex eligibility calculations and completion of forms for Chapter 33 claims.  Chapter 33 LTS is used to establish eligibility, determine payment, or disallow a claim should eligibility requirements or some other validating factor not be met. Automation of Chapter 33 claims benefits the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) and Veterans by providing efficient and streamlined determination of eligibility, accurate calculation of payments, and timely distribution of benefits to the Veterans and to the institutions that serve Veteran education needs.

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training
heading_path: Sub-Chapter 5. Distance Learning, Independent Study, and Internet Training
retrieved_chunks.content:
Any courses that consist of interaction using communications technology and/or some standard class sessions, but do not meet the requirements of in-residence training, are considered independent study or distance learning.

b. Benefit Payment

Distance learning, independent study, and Internet training are generally offered by Institutions of Higher Learning (such as colleges and universities), and similar rules and rates apply for tuition and fees, monthly housing allowance, books and supplies stipend, and Yellow Ribbon benefits.

c. Housing Allowance

The rules for housing benefits for veterans enrolled in independent training, distance learning, or Internet training are slightly different from the rules for veterans at IHLs.

Under the Post-9/11 GI Bill, the individual must be enrolled in at least one “in-residence” course to receive the full monthly housing allowance based on the Department of Defense’s (DoD’s) Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) rate for the school’s ZIP Code.

As of October 1, 2011, individuals are eligible to receive a monthly housing allowance based on one-half of the national average of DoD’s BAH rates if enrolled solely in distance learning, independent training, or Internet training.

Note: As for the other training programs, monthly housing allowance eligibility requires a Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual’s benefit level.

Sources

Electronic Code of Federal Regulations, § 21.9590 Approved programs of education and courses.

Electronic Code of Federal Regulations, § 21.9505 Definitions.

Policy Advisory: Independent Study/Distance Learning vs. Resident Training, March 26, 2010

PL 111-377, Post-9/11 Veterans Educational Assistance Improvements Act of 2010, Title II Other Educational Assistance Matters, Section 105. Educational Assistance for Programs of Education Other than Programs of Education Leading to a Degree

Training Slides PL111-377

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, under Chapter 33 (Post-9/11 GI Bill), a veteran taking **only distance learning courses** will be paid a Monthly Housing Allowance (MHA) based on **50% (one-half) of the national average of the BAH rates payable in the U.S.** This rate has been in effect since **October 1, 2011**. [1][5]

> **Important note:** Monthly housing allowance eligibility also requires a Rate of Pursuit (RoP) of more than .50, and benefits are pro-rated based on the RoP and the individual's benefit level. [5]

results.query: How is MHA calculated for a part-time student enrolled in 9 credits where 12 is full time?

6.04  Undergraduate Residence Training--Full time is 12 Credit Hours
retrieved_chunks.content:
a.  Introduction

This paragraph covers how to determine the correct measurement in the most common situations.  The assumptions are:

     all courses earn college credit

     12 credits is full time

     all courses are taken in residence

     the student is enrolled in a standard quarter or semester.

Reference:  38 CFR 21.4270(c)

b.  School certification

Schools will report the number of credit hours of residence training on the enrollment certification as shown in paragraph 6.01e.

c.  Approval record

The OLAF EO1 screen (see par. 6.02b) will show "12" in the FULL-TIME MEASUREMENT: UNDERGRADUATE field.

d.  Procedure

Use the following table to determine training time if 12 credit hours is full time.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 11 - Section 901
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER II.  INQUIRY, AWARD, AND DISALLOWANCE PROCESSING
retrieved_chunks.content:
(1)  Credit Hour Measurement - Standard Term - Undergraduate:  Twelve or more undergraduate hours (semester or quarter), will constitute full-time training.  Any number of credit hours less than 12 will constitute part-time training.

(2)  Credit Hour Measurement - Nonstandard Term - Undergraduate:  Determine this training time as follows:

(i)  Multiply the credits earned in the term by 18 if credit hours are semester, or by 12 if credit hours are quarter; and

(ii)  Divide the product by the number of whole weeks in the term.

In determining whole weeks:

Divide the number of days in the term by 7;

Disregard a remainder of 3 days or less; and

Consider 4 days or more to be a whole week.

(iii)  If the result obtained by using the formula in subparagraphs (a) and (b) above is 12 or more, the training time will be full time.  If the result is less than 12, the training time is part time.

(3)  Credit Hour Measurement - Graduate

(i)  If the established policy of a school is to determine the training time for a graduate student, VA will accept the school's certification.

(ii)  If the school does not have an established policy in this matter, VA will measure the student's enrollment according to subparagraph (1) or (2).  This policy has the effect of not distinguishing between graduate or undergraduate training.  If the student is taking both types of courses concurrently, VA will combine the credit hours of the courses as reported by the school and calculate the equivalent training time.  (See Part 4: Chapter 6 - Measurement of College Level Courses .)

(4)  Clock Hour Measurement

(i)  If the student enrolls in a course measured in clock hours and if shop practice is an integral part of the course, he or she is considered a full-time student when enrolled for 22 or more clock hours with not more than a 2-hour rest period.  If enrolled for less than 22 clock

results.answer:
## MHA Calculation for Part-Time Student (9 of 12 Credits)

### Step 1: Determine Training Time
A student enrolled in **9 through 11 credit hours** (where 12 is full time) is at **3/4 time** training. [5]

### Step 2: Calculate Rate of Pursuit (RoP)
Divide the enrolled credit hours by the full-time credit hours:

> **9 ÷ 12 = 0.75 (75%)**

### Step 3: Calculate MHA
Per policy effective **October 1, 2011**:

> *"MHA is calculated by multiplying the BAH rate by the rate of pursuit and rounding to the nearest multiple of 10."* [1]

So the formula is:

> **Applicable BAH Rate × 0.75 = MHA (rounded to nearest $10)**

### Example
If the applicable BAH rate is **$2,000/month**:
- $2,000 × 0.75 = **$1,500/month MHA**

The student would receive **75% of the full BAH rate**, rounded to the nearest multiple of $10. [1][5]

results.query: What benefit level does a veteran with 800 days of qualifying active-duty service receive under Chapter 33?
results.expected_answer: 80% of maximum benefit (730-909 days qualifies for 80%, subject to the 70/80 rule based on training exclusion)

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Effective October 1, 2011, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under title 32. Payments may be made retroactively for enrollment(s) at an approved educational institution for attendance on or after August 1, 2009. The following service may be qualifying:

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
heading_path: 4.02 EXTENSIONS BASED ON A LATER PERIOD OF ACTIVE DUTY
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. General. Typically, individuals eligible under Chapters 30, 32 or Section 903 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 10 years from date of discharge; however, a later period of qualifying active duty service may give the individual a new 10-year delimiting period for these benefits.  Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

Typically, individuals eligible under Chapter 33 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 15 years from date of discharge; however, a later qualifying period of active duty service may give the individual a new 15-year delimiting period for this benefit. The period of eligibility for Chapter 33 is based on the individual’s last date of discharge from any qualifying period.  VCEs should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

NOTE: If the individual’s date of discharge is after January 1, 2013, the individual will have no delimiting date under Chapter 33.

b. Length of Later Period of Active Duty. For purposes of giving an individual a new period of eligibility (and a new delimiting date), a later period of active duty must be at least 90 days in length if it ended on or after December 18, 1989. The only exceptions to this 90-day requirement are for individuals who are discharged or released due to a service-connected disability, for a non-service connected medical condition which pre-existed the later active duty service, for hardship, or because of a Reduction in Force (RIF) for convenience of the government. (38 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 21.7050(a), 21.5041(a), 21.5745(a)) Under Chapter 33, only a service-connected disablity discharge may be for a period less than 90 continious days. (38 USC 3321)

NOTE:VA’s General Counsel determined VA is not extending the delimiting date, but actually granting a new eligibility period beginning on the date of the claimant’s last release from active duty. Consequently, if the member re-enters active duty after his original delimiting date has passed, that member would not qualify for benefits until their 90th day back on active duty. The new 10-year eligibility period would begin once the claimant is released from active duty for the subsequent period.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits
heading_path: Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits
3.11  SPECIFIC ISSUES REGARDING VERIFICATION OF SERVICE
b. Full-time Duty in the Reserve or National Guard.
retrieved_chunks.content:
In some instances full-time duty in the Reserve or National Guard may be considered active duty under Title 38. For Chapter 30, see Part 5 Chapter 1 - General . National Guard members and Reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to received Chapter 33 benefits. For additional information regarding Chapter 33 benefits, refer to  Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans paragraph 2.01(c).

(1) Duty Status of Reservists. At the beginning of an enlistment in the Reserves, an individual is required to perform Initial Active Duty for Training (IADT) for a period of not less than 4 months. The remainder of the enlistment is spent as a member of the Ready Reserves.

A. Active or Inactive Duty for Training. Title 38 CFR 3.6 describes active and inactive duty for training. If a Reservist's only service is active or inactive duty for training, the Reservist must have died or sustained a disability during the period of training to be considered a Veteran. If the period of training did not result in death or disability, the Reservist is not a Veteran based on that service.

B. Full-time Duty in the Uniformed Services. Since the 1960's the Reserve components have had several programs such as the Active Guard Reserve (AGR) and the Active Duty Support (ADS) Program in which members serve full-time in operational or support positions but are never formally called to active duty. This type of service, whether it lasts one day or 3 years, is classified by the service departments as "active duty for training (ACDUTRA)."

The term "full-time duty in the uniformed services" is not defined in 38 U.S.C or 38 CFR. However, an opinion issued by the General Counsel on November 9, 1988, makes it clear that, despite the military's ACDUTRA classification, VA has the authority to declare certain types of service performed by Reservists to be "active duty" for the purposes of VA benefits. According to this opinion, Reservists meet the definition of active duty if the facts of record establish that the service was full-time and was for operational or support (as opposed to training) purposes.

Certain types of Reservist duty are generally NOT "full-time duty in the armed forces." These include the following:

(a) IADT (Except for Chapter 33 depending upon total aggregate service)

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits
heading_path: Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits
retrieved_chunks.content:
Ex: Beneficiary originally enlisted for six years of active-duty service, served from January 1, 2018, to December 31, 2024, and was released from active duty with an Honorable character of discharge and separation reason of Expiration Term of Service. Chapter 33 is requested on an application dated August 1, 2025.

36 months of active-duty service is required to establish chapter 33 at 100%, and 30 months of active-duty service is required to establish chapter 30 due to the ETS separation. The beneficiary served a total of 72 months, meaning they can establish full entitlement to both benefits without crediting the same day of service to both benefits. To provide full retroactive coverage at the greatest benefit level under the benefit requested (chapter 33), apply the first 36 months of service to chapter 33, January 1, 2018, to December 31, 2020, establishing 100% benefit level as of December 31, 2020. Apply the remainder of the service to chapter 30, January 1, 2021, to December 31, 2024.

If a beneficiary has sufficient service to establish entitlement under both benefits but insufficient service to establish full entitlement, develop to the beneficiary for assignment of periods of time in service to each benefit. In most cases, this election will be the choice to receive full chapter 30 entitlement, plus chapter 33 entitlement at a reduced benefit level, or full chapter 33 entitlement and no chapter 30 entitlement. Covered discharges may allow for full chapter 33 entitlement and reduced chapter 30 entitlement. In the future, Education Service plans to collect this election during the application process if it is determined the beneficiary has insufficient service to establish full entitlement to both benefits.

Claims Processing Scenarios

Veteran is eligible for chapter 33 and chapter 1606.

An election to give up chapter 1606 benefits is no longer required to establish eligibility under chapter 33. The beneficiary may wish to voluntarily relinquish chapter 1606 benefits to receive a chapter 1606 kicker under chapter 33.

    1. Veteran is eligible for a chapter 1606 kicker:
      1. No alternative election is needed. Process claim with no relinquishment.
      2. If chapter 1606 was previously used, enter the amount of entitlement used into DGI, but do not check the “Eligible” checkbox.

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
heading_path: 2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons
retrieved_chunks.content: All benefit payments are based on the amount of qualifying active duty service each Veteran has after September 10, 2001. The benefit level chart outlines the percentage of benefits based on qualifying service. It applies to active duty, National Guard, or Selected Reserve members, who have served on active duty for at least 90 days.

context_strings:
[Chunk 1] Source: 554400000073736 — Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans Heading: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans > 2.01 Qualifying Service2.01 Qualifying Service

a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Effective October 1, 2011, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under title 32. Payments may be made retroactively for enrollment(s) at an approved educational institution for attendance on or after August 1, 2009. The following service may be qualifying:

[Chunk 2] Source: 554400000073604 — Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions Heading: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions > 4.02 EXTENSIONS BASED ON A LATER PERIOD OF ACTIVE DUTY

a. General. Typically, individuals eligible under Chapters 30, 32 or Section 903 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 10 years from date of discharge; however, a later period of qualifying active duty service may give the individual a new 10-year delimiting period for these benefits.  Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

Typically, individuals eligible under Chapter 33 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 15 years from date of discharge; however, a later qualifying period of active duty service may give the individual a new 15-year delimiting period for this benefit. The period of eligibility for Chapter 33 is based on the individual’s last date of discharge from any qualifying period.  VCEs should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

NOTE: If the individual’s date of discharge is after January 1, 2013, the individual will have no delimiting date under Chapter 33.

b. Length of Later Period of Active Duty. For purposes of giving an individual a new period of eligibility (and a new delimiting date), a later period of active duty must be at least 90 days in length if it ended on or after December 18, 1989. The only exceptions to this 90-day requirement are for individuals who are discharged or released due to a service-connected disability, for a non-service connected medical condition which pre-existed the later active duty service, for hardship, or because of a Reduction in Force (RIF) for convenience of the government. (38 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 21.7050(a), 21.5041(a), 21.5745(a)) Under Chapter 33, only a service-connected disablity discharge may be for a period less than 90 continious days. (38 USC 3321)

NOTE:VA’s General Counsel determined VA is not extending the delimiting date, but actually granting a new eligibility period beginning on the date of the claimant’s last release from active duty. Consequently, if the member re-enters active duty after his original delimiting date has passed, that member would not qualify for benefits until their 90th day back on active duty. The new 10-year eligibility period would begin once the claimant is released from active duty for the subsequent period.

[Chunk 3] Source: 554400000073583 — Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits Heading: Part 3: Chapter 3 - Processing Applications for Benefits > SUBCHAPTER II.  GENERAL PROCEDURES > 3.11  SPECIFIC ISSUES REGARDING VERIFICATION OF SERVICE > b. Full-time Duty in the Reserve or National Guard.

In some instances full-time duty in the Reserve or National Guard may be considered active duty under Title 38. For Chapter 30, see Part 5 Chapter 1 - General . National Guard members and Reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to received Chapter 33 benefits. For additional information regarding Chapter 33 benefits, refer to  Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans paragraph 2.01(c).

(1) Duty Status of Reservists. At the beginning of an enlistment in the Reserves, an individual is required to perform Initial Active Duty for Training (IADT) for a period of not less than 4 months. The remainder of the enlistment is spent as a member of the Ready Reserves.

A. Active or Inactive Duty for Training. Title 38 CFR 3.6 describes active and inactive duty for training. If a Reservist's only service is active or inactive duty for training, the Reservist must have died or sustained a disability during the period of training to be considered a Veteran. If the period of training did not result in death or disability, the Reservist is not a Veteran based on that service.

B. Full-time Duty in the Uniformed Services. Since the 1960's the Reserve components have had several programs such as the Active Guard Reserve (AGR) and the Active Duty Support (ADS) Program in which members serve full-time in operational or support positions but are never formally called to active duty. This type of service, whether it lasts one day or 3 years, is classified by the service departments as "active duty for training (ACDUTRA)."

The term "full-time duty in the uniformed services" is not defined in 38 U.S.C or 38 CFR. However, an opinion issued by the General Counsel on November 9, 1988, makes it clear that, despite the military's ACDUTRA classification, VA has the authority to declare certain types of service performed by Reservists to be "active duty" for the purposes of VA benefits. According to this opinion, Reservists meet the definition of active duty if the facts of record establish that the service was full-time and was for operational or support (as opposed to training) purposes.

Certain types of Reservist duty are generally NOT "full-time duty in the armed forces." These include the following:

(a) IADT (Except for Chapter 33 depending upon total aggregate service)

    1. Veteran is eligible for a chapter 1606 kicker:
      1. No alternative election is needed. Process claim with no relinquishment.
      2. If chapter 1606 was previously used, enter the amount of entitlement used into DGI, but do not check the “Eligible” checkbox.

For a veteran with 800 days (~26 months) of qualifying active-duty service, the context states that "less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a **lower percentage of benefits**" [1], and that "all benefit payments are based on the amount of qualifying active duty service each Veteran has after September 10, 2001" [5].

The chunks reference a "benefit level chart" that outlines the percentage of benefits based on qualifying service [5], but that chart itself is not included in the provided context.

I don't know based on the provided context what the exact percentage would be for 800 days of service, as the benefit level chart is not reproduced in the available chunks.

results.query: What happened to the 40% benefit level for Chapter 33?
results.expected_answer: Eliminated effective August 1, 2020; became the 50% benefit level

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
heading_path: 2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons
retrieved_chunks.content:
*The 70/80% rule is as follows:

If the aggregate service including training is at least 24 months (730-909 days) but less than 30 months (910-1094 days) AND the aggregate service excluding training is at least 18 months (545 to 729 days) but less than 24 months, the individual will be deemed eligible at the 70% benefit level. See Sub-Chapter 5 Eligibility Excluded Service.

Note: Historically, a 40% benefit level existed for individuals with at least 90 days, but less than 6 months of service. The 40% benefit level was eliminated, effective August 1, 2020, and became the 50% benefit level. In a corresponding change, the previous service requirements for 50% benefit level (at least 6 months but less than 12 months) and 60% benefit level (at least 12 months but less than 18 months) were consolidated, with the service requirement for 60% benefit level becoming at least 6 months but less than 18 months. Individuals previously eligible at the 40% benefit level are eligible to receive payment at the 50% benefit level for enrollments beginning on or after August 1, 2020.

Sources

PL 111-377, Post-9/11 Veterans Educational Assistance Improvements Act of 2010, Title II Other Educational Assistance Matters, Section 101. Modification of Entitlement to Educational Assistance

An individual's entitlement under chapter 34 (38 CFR 21.1041) affects his or her potential eligibility under chapter 30 as a category II person and, if eligible, his or her chapter 30 monthly rate.  To be eligible for chapter 30 benefits as a category II individual, he or she must have at least one day of remaining chapter 34 entitlement as of December 31, 1989.  Such an individual's remaining entitlement as of December 31, 1989, is used as a basis for increasing his or her chapter 30 monthly rate.  (See ch. 1.)  Also, a category II person's chapter 30 entitlement may be reduced based on the 48-month limitation on benefits a person may receive under more than one VA education benefit program.  (See par. 9.06b.)

retrieved_chunks.title: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.5 - Additional Benefits
retrieved_chunks.content:
For training prior to August 1, 2011,

•  Eligible individuals receive payments for a Chapter 30 or Chapter 1606 kicker in a lump sum for the entire quarter, semester, or term, as applicable,

•  Kickers are paid based on the monthly amount to which the individual was entitled at the time of the election of Chapter 33 (see NOTE below),

•  The amount is prorated by the rate of pursuit for less than full time training.
 

 
For training September 30, 2011,

•  Eligible individuals receive payments for a Chapter 30 or Chapter 1606 kicker on a monthly, rather than a lump sum, basis. [Note: Eligibility for the monthly housing allowance isn’t required], 

•  Kickers are paid based on the monthly amount to which the individual was entitled at the time of the election of Chapter 33 (see NOTE below),

•  The amount is prorated by the rate of pursuit rounded to the nearest multiple of 10 for less than full time training.

NOTE:  Individuals relinquishing Chapter 1606 for Chapter 33 shall be paid their 1606 kicker in the same manner payable as if using Chapter 1606. The amount payable may be suspended, increase, decrease or be terminated by DoD. A Chapter 1606 kicker is no longer payable after separation from the Selected Reserves. VCEs must review the Veterans Information System (VIS) for changes in eligibility and develop with the service component only when information reported to VA is conflicting. (e.g. Military Status indicates “Separated” or the RCC code on the Military History page is “RE” indicating IRR service but 1606 eligibility has not been terminated or suspended.)


Sources:

Continuation of entitlement to additional educational assistance for critical skills or specialty.

The beneficiary must meet all the requirements listed below.  If any do not apply, the beneficiary is not entitled to a refund, therefore, no further adjudicative action is required.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 4: Chapter 1 - Enrollment Certification
heading_path: SUBCHAPTER I.  GENERAL INFORMATION REGARDING ENROLLMENT CERTIFICATIONS
retrieved_chunks.content:
• I may not receive more than a total of 48 months of benefits under two or more programs.
  • If electing chapter 33 in lieu of chapter 30, my months of entitlement under chapter 33 will be limited to the number of months of entitlement remaining under chapter 30 on the effective date of my election.  However, if I completely exhaust my entitlement under chapter 30 before the effective date of my Chapter 33 election, I may receive up to 12 additional months of benefits under chapter 33.  CAUTION: Public Law 111-377 does not allow service that starts August 1, 2011 or later to be credited towards more than one benefit.
  • My election is irrevocable and may not be changed.

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, the 40% benefit level was **eliminated effective August 1, 2020**, and became the **50% benefit level**. [1]

Specifically, the following changes occurred:

- The **40% benefit level** (previously for individuals with at least 90 days but less than 6 months of service) was eliminated and rolled into the **50% benefit level**
- The previous **50% benefit level** (at least 6 months but less than 12 months) and **60% benefit level** (at least 12 months but less than 18 months) were **consolidated**, with the 60% benefit level's service requirement becoming **at least 6 months but less than 18 months**
- Individuals previously eligible at the 40% benefit level are eligible to receive payment at the **50% benefit level for enrollments beginning on or after August 1, 2020** [1]

results.query: Does a veteran discharged on February 1, 2014, have a delimiting date for Chapter 33 benefits?
results.expected_answer: No. Veterans discharged on or after January 1, 2013 have no time limit to use Chapter 33 benefits (per PL 115-48, effective August 18, 2017)

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
heading_path: 4.05 EXTENSION FOR POW OR MIA STATUS
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. POW or MIA Status. If a Chapter 33 or Chapter 30 Veteran is detained by a foreign government or power after his or her last discharge or release from active duty, VA may extend the delimiting date by the length of time the Veteran was detained. This extension may include any period immediately after release from captivity during which the Veteran was hospitalized in a civilian, military or VA medical facility. 

This applies to claimants receiving Chapter 33 Fry benefits if the Veteran was in either POW or MIA status as well.

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Individuals eligible under the various laws described in this manual have a period of eligibility during which they must use their entitlement. The first day after an individual's period of eligibility expires is known as his or her delimiting date.

Effective August 18, 2017, per Public Law 115-48, individuals who qualify under Chapter 33 benefits and separate from Active Duty with an Honorable discharge after January 1, 2013, do not have a period of eligibility to complete their education benefits.  These individuals do not have a delimiting date and are, therefore, not subject to delimiting date restrictions. 

b. An individual's period of eligibility may be extended under certain situations. Extensions to an individual's period of eligibility are frequently called "delimiting date extensions." Figure 4.01 provides a summary of the various types of delimiting date extensions for claimants under Chapters 33, 30, 32, 35, 1606, 1607, and Section 903. Other paragraphs in this chapter explain each type of extension.

c. Reservists receiving benefits under Chapter 1606 do not have a delimiting date if they remain actively drilling.  Once they are no longer drilling, they are no longer entitled to benefits and do not have a period of eligibility unless one of the specific situations outlined below occurs.

NOTE: In Ozer v. Principi, the United States Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC) decided Chapter 35 surviving spouses do not have a delimiting date. Prior to the Ozer decision, both spouses and surviving spouses had a 10-year window to use their Chapter 35 benefits.  The Ozer decision was invalidated by Public Law 107-103, which reinstated the delimiting date for spouses effective December 28, 2001. Therefore, a delimiting date extension remains an issue for Chapter 35 spouses. Those affected by the Ozer decision, continue to not have a delimiting date as long as they have not changed their program.

 

 Figure 4.01. Applicability of Delimiting Date Extensions

retrieved_chunks.title: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions
heading_path: 4.06 EXTENSION FOR UPGRADED DISCHARGE/ RECORDS CORRECTION
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. General. Service members who use VA education benefits while on active duty and Veterans who were potentially eligible, but chose not to use their benefits, while awaiting service correction or upgraded characterization, would not be eligible for a delimiting date extension under this section. (38 CFR 21.9625(e), 21.7050(f))

b. Qualifications. A Veteran with one period of service, who either (1) has never applied for VA education benefits or (2) was previously denied by VA in the past, may become eligible as a result of the correction of his or her military records and/or upgrade of their character of discharge. (10 USC 1552,1553)

c. Chapter 33 Veterans. Depending upon the release from active duty (RAD) date of the service period in question, Veterans applying for benefits under the Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33) may be processed using different means, as described below.

NOTE 1: A Veteran whose service previously established eligibility under Chapter 33 is not entitled to a delimiting date extension under this section. If eligibility had been previously established, the beneficiary will retain their original eligibility period, unless other action, outside of a military records correction or discharge upgrade, allows for an extension.

NOTE 2: An upgraded discharge, for service ending prior to January 1, 2013, will remain subject to 38 USC 3321(a)(1), still requiring a 15-year eligibility period beginning upon the date of the upgrade.

Example: An individual served on active duty from January 17, 2006, to September 13, 2012. They were released with a character of discharge of “Under Honorable Conditions” (UHC) with a separation reason of “Other”. Their service characterization was upgraded to “Honorable” on June 10, 2015. The individual would be eligible under Chapter 33 until their delimiting date on June 10, 2030.

Currently, the Chapter 33 Processing System (DGI) does not allow for data entry to indicate a change in Character of Service to a fully Honorable status.  Consequently, DGI does not generate the correct Date of Eligibility or Delimiting Date (when one is required).

Workaround. Until DGI is modified to allow for a military records correction or an upgrade to the character of service, Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should:

context_strings:
[Chunk 1] Source: 554400000073736 — Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans Heading: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans > 2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons2.02 Length of Service and Separation Reasons > 2.04 Period of Eligibility

a. Veterans who qualify for chapter 33 have up to 15 years to use their education benefits if discharged prior to January 1, 2013. Veterans discharged on or after January 1, 2013, have no time limit to use benefits.

Eligibility for education benefits begins on the date of the last active-duty discharge of either:

[Chunk 2] Source: 554400000073604 — Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions Heading: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions > 4.05 EXTENSION FOR POW OR MIA STATUS

a. POW or MIA Status. If a Chapter 33 or Chapter 30 Veteran is detained by a foreign government or power after his or her last discharge or release from active duty, VA may extend the delimiting date by the length of time the Veteran was detained. This extension may include any period immediately after release from captivity during which the Veteran was hospitalized in a civilian, military or VA medical facility. 

This applies to claimants receiving Chapter 33 Fry benefits if the Veteran was in either POW or MIA status as well.

[Chunk 3] Source: 554400000073604 — Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions Heading: Part 3: Chapter 4 - Delimiting Date Extensions > 4.02 EXTENSIONS BASED ON A LATER PERIOD OF ACTIVE DUTY

a. General. Typically, individuals eligible under Chapters 30, 32 or Section 903 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 10 years from date of discharge; however, a later period of qualifying active duty service may give the individual a new 10-year delimiting period for these benefits.  Veterans Claims Examiners (VCEs) should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

Typically, individuals eligible under Chapter 33 may receive education benefits for a period not to exceed 15 years from date of discharge; however, a later qualifying period of active duty service may give the individual a new 15-year delimiting period for this benefit. The period of eligibility for Chapter 33 is based on the individual’s last date of discharge from any qualifying period.  VCEs should verify this service in the same manner as any other period of service.

NOTE: If the individual’s date of discharge is after January 1, 2013, the individual will have no delimiting date under Chapter 33.

b. Length of Later Period of Active Duty. For purposes of giving an individual a new period of eligibility (and a new delimiting date), a later period of active duty must be at least 90 days in length if it ended on or after December 18, 1989. The only exceptions to this 90-day requirement are for individuals who are discharged or released due to a service-connected disability, for a non-service connected medical condition which pre-existed the later active duty service, for hardship, or because of a Reduction in Force (RIF) for convenience of the government. (38 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 21.7050(a), 21.5041(a), 21.5745(a)) Under Chapter 33, only a service-connected disablity discharge may be for a period less than 90 continious days. (38 USC 3321)

NOTE:VA’s General Counsel determined VA is not extending the delimiting date, but actually granting a new eligibility period beginning on the date of the claimant’s last release from active duty. Consequently, if the member re-enters active duty after his original delimiting date has passed, that member would not qualify for benefits until their 90th day back on active duty. The new 10-year eligibility period would begin once the claimant is released from active duty for the subsequent period.

results.answer:
No. A veteran discharged on **February 1, 2014** does **not** have a delimiting date for Chapter 33 benefits.

Per the policy language: *"individuals who qualify under Chapter 33 benefits and separate from Active Duty with an Honorable discharge after January 1, 2013, do not have a period of eligibility to complete their education benefits. These individuals do not have a delimiting date and are, therefore, not subject to delimiting date restrictions."* [4]

This is further confirmed: *"Veterans discharged on or after January 1, 2013, have no time limit to use benefits."* [1]

results.query: Is Title 32 AGR service qualifying for the Post-9/11 GI Bill?
results.expected_answer: Yes, effective October 1, 2011. AGR service under Title 32 for organizing, administering, recruiting, instructing, or training, and activations under Section 502(f) for national emergencies are qualifying. Retroactive payment is possible for enrollments on or after August 1, 2009.

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
retrieved_chunks.content:
If individuals affected by these changes were already using the Post-9/11 GI Bill previously, they will be paid for any additional benefits they would have been entitled to because of a higher benefit level.

In addition to the title 32 service in the previous section, mobilizations under the following titles and sections qualify as active duty service for the purpose of chapter 33:

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 1. Service Members
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. A service member is eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill education benefits if he or she served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or order to active duty from a selected reserve component (National Guard or Reserve) for title 10 mobilizations and under certain sections of Title 32.

b. Active Duty

Service members can use the Post-9/11 GI Bill on active duty as long as they have completed at least 90 days of qualifying service after September 10, 2001, and continue to be on active duty. This does not include basic entry-level and skill training if less than 24 months of active duty are accrued. The tuition payment is limited to the net out-of-pocket expenses after the deduction of certain types of 3rd party assistance including federal tuition assistance.

Back to top

retrieved_chunks.title: Sub-Chapter 2. Veterans
retrieved_chunks.content:
a. Veterans are eligible for Post-9/11 GI Bill Education Benefits if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability or sole survivorship. This covers certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32.

Basic entry-level and skills training does not count towards qualifying service unless the Veteran has completed 730 days (24 months) of service. For more information, see 70/80 rule below.

Note: 38 USC § 3301 and 38 CFR § 21.9505 specifically use the term “Armed Forces” when referencing qualifying active duty service, rather than “Uniformed Services”. VA General Counsel has determined the two terms have the same meaning for purposes of VA benefits due to 33 USC 3072(a) and 42 USC 213(d), stating service in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and Public Health Service, respectively, is considered active military service under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs. Therefore, the reference to “Armed Forces” in 38 USC § 3301 should be treated as having the same meaning as “Uniformed Services”.

b. Veterans

To qualify for chapter 33 benefits, a Veteran must have served at least 90 aggregate (total) days of active duty service after September 10, 2001, and have an honorable discharge. Three years of total service or a discharge due to a service-connected disability or sole survivorship will qualify a Veteran for 100% of the Post-9/11 GI Bill benefit. Less than 3 years of cumulative service will qualify a Veteran for a lower percentage of benefits.

c. National Guard/Reserve

National Guard members and reservists who have served a minimum of 90 days of qualifying service are eligible to receive chapter 33 benefits.

Note: Effective October 1, 2011, VA began paying benefits to members of the Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under title 32. Payments may be made retroactively for enrollment(s) at an approved educational institution for attendance on or after August 1, 2009. The following service may be qualifying:

retrieved_chunks.title: Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 4.1 Overview
retrieved_chunks.content:
M22-4 / Part 12 – Chapter 33 / 04 – Monetary Benefits

Chapter 33 Sub-Chapter 1. Overview

a. General

The Post-9/11 GI Bill provides financial support for education and housing to individuals with at least 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, or at least 30 days if discharged for a service-connected disability. This includes certain types of active duty served as a member of the Uniformed Services or because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under certain sections of Title 10 or Title 32. 

b. Eligibility

Eligible service members, veterans, and dependents attending approved education and training programs are entitled to the following monetary benefits:

retrieved_chunks.title: Chapter 2. Eligibility
retrieved_chunks.content:
M22-4 / Part 12 – Chapter 33 / 02 – Eligibility

Chapter 2. Eligibility

This section outlines who is entitled to receive the Post-9/11 GI Bill Educational Assistance benefit.

The Post-9/11 GI Bill Educational Assistance (Chapter 33) program offers education benefits for service members and veterans who have served on active duty for at least 90 days after September 10, 2001. Benefit levels are determined by the number of days served on active duty or qualifying active duty mobilization(s) from a Reserve or National Guard unit. Members are eligible for benefits 15 years after their last date of discharge and may receive up to 36 months of chapter 33 benefits.

Please use this table to help find out more about eligibility.

Last updated: Monday, October 5, 2015

results.answer:
Based on the provided context, Title 32 service can be qualifying for the Post-9/11 GI Bill, but the context does not specifically address **Title 32 AGR (Active Guard and Reserve)** service in detail.

What the context does confirm is:

- Veterans are eligible if they served a minimum of 90 days on active duty after September 10, 2001, including service "because of a call up or mobilization to active duty from a reserve component (National Guard or Selected Reserve) under **certain sections of title 10, title 14, or title 32**." [3]

- Effective **October 1, 2011**, VA began paying benefits to members of the **Army National Guard and Air National Guard based on qualifying service under Title 32**, with retroactive payments possible for enrollment on or after **August 1, 2009**. [3]

- The Post-9/11 GI Bill covers "certain types of active duty...because of a call up or order to active duty from a selected reserve component (National Guard or Reserve) for title 10 mobilizations and **under certain sections of Title 32**." [1][2]

However, the context does not enumerate which **specific sections** of Title 32 qualify, nor does it explicitly address AGR status under Title 32.

**I don't know based on the provided context** whether Title 32 AGR service specifically qualifies.

results.query: Can a veteran use GI Bill benefits for an avocational or recreational course?
results.expected_answer: Avocational or recreational courses are presumed prohibited under chapters 30, 32, and 35; the course may only be paid for if there is evidence establishing that the course is of bona fide use in pursuit of the veteran's vocational objective.
